childCalories.name: Child Calorie Needs Calculator
childCalories.description: Daily calorie needs for children (ages 1–18) by age, sex, height, weight and activity level — IOM formula.

meta.title: Child Calorie Needs Calculator — Daily Calories for Kids
meta.description: Calculate your child's daily calorie needs (ages 1–18) by age, sex, height, weight and activity level. IOM reference values. Free, instant.

childCalories.title: Child Calorie Needs Calculator
childCalories.description: How many calories does your child need per day? Calculation by age, sex, height, weight and activity level.
childCalories.implausibleAge: Please enter an age between 1 and 18 years.
childCalories.implausibleWeight: Please enter a realistic weight between 5 and 150 kg.
childCalories.implausibleHeight: Please enter a realistic height between 60 and 220 cm.
childCalories.toddlerNote: For toddlers (ages 1–2), the calculator uses the fixed IOM reference values — height and weight are not used.
childCalories.tableNote: Values for moderate activity, IOM reference.
childCalories.howItWorksText: The calculator uses the Estimated Energy Requirement (EER) formula from the U.S. Institute of Medicine (IOM). It accounts for age, sex, height, weight and an age-specific physical activity coefficient (PA). For children under three, fixed reference values apply (1-year-old boy: 948 kcal, 2-year-old girl: 992 kcal etc.). From age 3: kcal/day = constants − age + PA·(weight + height). Growth spurts, puberty and exercise change needs significantly.
childCalories.disclaimer: Important: these are guideline values. Growth, illness and activity vary widely. If you are concerned about under- or overeating, talk to your pediatrician or a registered dietitian.

faq.q: How many calories does a child need per day?
faq.a: It depends heavily on age. A 4-year-old girl with moderate activity needs around 1300 kcal, a 14-year-old active boy around 2800 kcal. The calculator uses the IOM EER formula for precise values.
The Estimated Energy Requirement (EER) from the U.S. Institute of Medicine (DRI 2002/2005). It is the standard in pediatrics and nutrition science and accounts for growth.

faq.q: What do the activity levels mean?
faq.a: Sedentary = school plus homework, little sport. Low active = walking to school plus outdoor play. Active = club sports several times a week. Very active = competitive sport or several hours of movement daily.

faq.q: Why does my child need more calories than me?
faq.a: Children grow — building muscle and bone, plus a higher resting metabolic rate per kg of body weight, raises energy needs. During puberty, requirements rise again significantly.

faq.q: Should I count calories for my child?
faq.a: No. For healthy children, focus on balanced meals and hunger cues. The calculator helps you estimate the order of magnitude — useful when there are concerns about weight or unusual activity levels.
No. Babies under 1 year follow different recommendations — see our Baby Feeding Amount Calculator. From age 1, this Child Calorie Needs Calculator applies.
